metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'impact-graded criterion' precisely in Section A / AC[3] — specify the exact routing attribute or criterion-id set the permissive-invariant test checks against, and reconcile with the 9 plan-review criteria (F1, E2, G5, COH, T5e, T4, T8, T1, G1G2) that are currently `default_posture: blocking` in `criteria_routing.json`.
findings.finding: The plan's permissive-rollout invariant claim — 'in both routing configs every impact-graded (LLM) criterion is default_posture: advisory at block_threshold 0.95' — is refuted by plan_review/criteria_routing.json, which contains at least 9 criteria with default_posture:'blocking': F1 (0.6), E2 (0.6), G5 (0.6), COH (0.6), T5e (0.6), T4 (0.7), T8 (0.7), T1 (0.7), G1G2 (0.7). The term 'impact-graded' is undefined in the routing schema (plan-review has no such field), so the proposed test that 'asserts no impact-graded criterion is a hard block' has no precise executable definition and may silently pass or check nothing meaningful.
findings.suggested_fix: Restate the AC as: 'a unit test asserts that no criterion in plan_review/criteria_routing.json with exec∈{AGENT,2-STEP,1-TURN} (i.e. non-DET LLM criteria) has default_posture:blocking AND a block_threshold below X' — or alternatively confirm that the invariant only applies to code-review impact criteria (where blocking_enabled:true criteria are correctly limited to DET detectors) and remove the plan-review half of the claim.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Redefine the AC3 plan-review invariant precisely: replace 'no non-advisory impact criterion' with 'no criterion whose *only* exec path runs the new impact_plan/impact_code model AND was not already `default_posture: blocking` before this child' (i.e., assert that THIS CHILD introduces zero new blocking criteria), and update the test spec to check the diff against a known-good snapshot of the pre-child routing rather than asserting a universal 'no blocking LLM criteria' claim that is false today.
findings.finding: The permissive-rollout invariant in AC3 uses the undefined term 'impact-graded criterion' for plan-review, leading to an untestable or factually incorrect assertion. Plan-review routes ALL LLM criteria through `impact_plan`, and the routing file already has many `default_posture: blocking` LLM criteria (F1, E2, G5, etc.). The plan simultaneously claims 'plan-review has no non-advisory impact criterion' — which is false per the committed `criteria_routing.json` — and asks a test to assert it. A test implementing the AC literally would either fail against the existing routing or be scoped so narrowly ('no criterion newly added by this child is blocking') that the AC text doesn't express it.
findings.location: Section A — Permissive rollout / AC3: 'plan-review: no non-advisory impact criterion'
findings.suggested_fix: In AC3, replace 'plan-review: no non-advisory impact criterion' with 'plan-review: this child introduces no new blocking_enabled/default_posture:blocking criteria; the only blocking criteria in both routing configs are the pre-existing ones (enumerated by their IDs or verified against a committed baseline snapshot).' The test then asserts the CURRENT routing's blocking set equals a pinned expected set, so any accidental addition is caught.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'impact-graded criterion' as a closed, schema-grounded tag (e.g. a `"graded_by": "impact_model"` field in the routing JSON entries, or an explicit allowlist constant) so the AC-line-3 invariant test can select exactly the right criteria without hard-coding IDs.
findings.finding: The plan's permissive-rollout invariant test (Section A / AC line 3) requires asserting 'no impact-graded criterion is a hard block,' but 'impact-graded (LLM) criterion' is never defined as a closed set or tagged in either routing JSON. The plan_review criteria_routing.json has multiple LLM-exec criteria with default_posture:'blocking' (F1, E2, G5, COH, T1, T5e, E5, E6, G6 at thresholds of 0.6–0.7) that are legitimately blocking but NOT impact-model criteria — a test implementer has no schema-grounded vocabulary to distinguish 'impact-graded' from 'structural/quality-graded' blocking criteria.
findings.location: Section A — permissive rollout / AC line 3: 'a test asserts no impact-graded criterion is a hard block in either packaged routing'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `"impact_model": true` boolean key to the routing-JSON entries for the LLM criteria that flow through impact_plan/impact_code (e.g. all non-DET, non-structural criteria at block_threshold 0.95), and have the invariant test assert `all(not entry.get('blocking_enabled', False) and entry.get('default_posture') == 'advisory' for entry in routing if entry.get('impact_model'))`. Alternatively, document in the AC the exact criterion-ID allowlist the test must use.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC E: Add a proving command that asserts the ADR file exists and contains required section headings, e.g. `test -f docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md && grep -q 'ship permissive' docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md`.
findings.finding: AC E (ADR) has no proving command. The criterion states 'A recalibration-cadence ADR is recorded under `docs/adr/`' but supplies neither a file-existence check nor a content assertion, making it unverifiable via the same pytest invocation used for ACs A–D.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC E ('A recalibration-cadence ADR is recorded under `docs/adr/`.')
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC E: 'Proving command: `test -f docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md && grep -qE "ship permissive|version-tagged|A/B gate" docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit-test scenario for `ab_impact_model.py`'s gate **failure path**: given a synthetic fixture where the new model's HIGH↔NIT separation does NOT strictly beat the baseline's, assert the script exits non-zero (e.g. use `pytest.raises(SystemExit)` or `subprocess` with a crafted fixture that inverts the scores).
findings.finding: The A/B harness gate's FAILURE path (sep_new <= sep_baseline → exit non-zero) has no corresponding test; the AC only verifies the passing case ('gate passes for the current fixture'), leaving the gate's enforcement behavior untested.
findings.location: Section D (A/B harness) and AC bullet 4: 'Unit-tested (the gate passes for the current fixture, since impact_code separates and the mean does not).'
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py`, add a parametrized test case that passes `ab_impact_model` a synthetic label fixture where all HIGH and NIT scores are identical (separation = 0 for both models), and asserts the returned exit code is non-zero. Complement it with a case where only the baseline separates but the new model does not (sep_new < sep_baseline) to cover the strict-beat condition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in Section D exactly how the baseline impact() is called on impact_code-centric fixture rows (e.g., 'passes the raw severity_attributes dict as-is, accepting that absent ordinal attrs map to 0.33 — which documents why the gap is large'), and add an explicit note that the baseline comparison is fixture-relative, not a general regression guard across future fixtures.
findings.finding: Section D's ab_impact_model.py specification does not define how the 'baseline mean impact()' function is called on fixture rows whose severity_attributes contain only impact_code consequence binaries (boolean keys like data_loss_without_recovery, dead_code, etc.) rather than the base ordinal attrs (prod_impact, debt_impact, blast_radius, likelihood, reversibility) that impact() reads. The kernel mean impact() returns a fixed 0.33 default for any missing ordinal attr — so for the majority of fixture rows (which have no ordinal attrs) the baseline is identically 0.33, making the gate trivially easy to beat. The plan does not specify whether the baseline call receives the raw severity_attributes dict as-is, or a transformed/augmented dict.
findings.location: Section D — A/B harness: 'scores the checked-in labeled/adjudicated set... with the NEW model (impact_code) and a BASELINE (the kernel mean impact)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add validation in `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py`: after applying the `--impact-model-version` filter, assert that at least one sidecar was loaded and exit non-zero with a clear message (e.g. "no sidecars matched version 'X' — known values: plan-v2, code-v2") if the result set is empty.
findings.finding: The `--impact-model-version` flag in `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py` performs no validation of the supplied version string. An unrecognized or misspelled version value (e.g. `--impact-model-version plan-v22`) will cause `load()` to skip ALL sidecars, silently delivering an empty dataset to the bucketing logic downstream — which can produce division-by-zero errors, misleading empty output, or a silent no-op rather than an actionable error message.
findings.suggested_fix: In `main()`, after `load()` returns, check `if not records: sys.exit(f"[calibrate] No sidecars matched --impact-model-version={args.impact_model_version!r}. Known versions: plan-v2, code-v2.")`. Optionally define an `KNOWN_VERSIONS` set and reject the flag value at argparse time via `choices=KNOWN_VERSIONS`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Section D / AC D: State the exact callable or attribute key that constitutes the 'baseline' (e.g. 'the scalar `impact` field already present in each fixture dict' or 'the function `rebar.llm.code_review.scoring.impact(attrs)`'), so the executor has a single unambiguous baseline implementation.
findings.finding: The A/B harness (Section D / AC D) describes the baseline as 'the kernel mean `impact`' without naming the actual callable, attribute key, or import path that the executor should use. An executor cannot implement `ab_impact_model.py` correctly without knowing whether this refers to a specific field in the fixture dict, a function in an existing module, or a computed mean of several numeric keys.
findings.location: Section D ('Diff-grounded A/B harness') and AC D
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to Section D such as: 'The BASELINE is the scalar `impact` key already present in each fixture dict (the pre-redesign single-kernel mean); `ab_impact_model.py` reads `row["impact"]` directly as the baseline score without calling any model function.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In Section D (and AC bullet 4), name the exact import path for the baseline callable, e.g. `from rebar.llm.review_kernel.decide import impact` (the original kernel-mean function at `decide.py:111`), so the implementer of `ab_impact_model.py` has an unambiguous target.
findings.finding: Section D describes the A/B harness baseline as 'the kernel mean `impact`' without citing the concrete import path (`rebar.llm.review_kernel.decide.impact` or `review_kernel.impact`). An implementer of `ab_impact_model.py` has no named import target in the plan, leaving the script's dependency on the baseline callable unspecified.
findings.location: Section D / AC bullet 4 ('scores … with the NEW model (`impact_code`) and a BASELINE (the kernel mean `impact`)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Section D: 'BASELINE: `rebar.llm.review_kernel.decide.impact` (the original kernel-mean formula, exported as `review_kernel.impact`)' and mirror this in AC bullet 4.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `--min-separation-margin` (or similar) argument to `ab_impact_model.py` (default `0.0` for current strict-beats semantics) so the gate threshold is a configurable, auditable policy parameter rather than a hardcoded comparison.
findings.finding: The A/B gate condition (`sep_new > sep_baseline`) is a business rule hardcoded in `ab_impact_model.py` with no provision for parameterization. This threshold may need to evolve (e.g., adding a minimum absolute margin, or a relative margin like `sep_new > sep_baseline * 1.05`) as the calibration discipline matures, but as specified it is a magic comparison baked into script logic.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `parser.add_argument('--min-separation-margin', type=float, default=0.0, help='new model separation must exceed baseline by at least this margin')` and replace the gate condition with `sep_new > sep_baseline + args.min_separation_margin`. Document the default in the ADR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `ab_impact_model.py` should be a thin CLI wrapper around the existing `_median_gap` helper in `test_impact_code.py` (or a shared utility it imports) rather than a full rebuild — if the only new value is the exit-code and human-readable report, extract a shared `median_gap(fn, fixture)` helper that both the test and the script invoke.
findings.finding: Section D's `ab_impact_model.py` reconstructs the same `_median_gap(impact_code) > _median_gap(impact)` computation already pinned as a production regression test in `tests/unit/test_impact_code.py:276-281` against the same fixture (`code_review_impact_labels.jsonl`). The script does not wrap or call the existing helper; it rebuilds it — a NIH duplication. The only new contribution is a CLI exit-code and human-readable report, which could be added to a thin wrapper or a `--report` flag on the existing script rather than a full rebuild.
findings.location: Section D / docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py (proposed new) vs tests/unit/test_impact_code.py:259-281
findings.suggested_fix: Extract a `rebar.llm.review_kernel.decide` (or `scripts/`) helper `median_gap(fn, fixture_path)` that both `test_impact_code.py::test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` and `ab_impact_model.py` import, so the gate logic has a single implementation. The A/B script then wraps it with argparse + exit-code + report output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In Section D / AC bullet 4, acknowledge that `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` in `test_impact_code.py` already covers the gap assertion, and clarify whether `test_impact_model_versioning.py` should call `ab_impact_model` as a subprocess / importable entrypoint (testing the script's exit code) rather than re-asserting the median-gap math.
findings.finding: `tests/unit/test_impact_code.py` already contains `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` (lines 276–281) which asserts `_median_gap(impact_code) > _median_gap(impact)` on the same `code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` fixture — the identical gate logic Section D / AC bullet 4 describe for the new `ab_impact_model.py` script and its unit test. The plan does not acknowledge this existing test as an out-of-scope overlap, leaving it unclear whether the new `test_impact_model_versioning.py` test should delegate to or duplicate that assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before adding `ab_impact_model.py`, document why the existing `_median_gap` / `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` in `test_impact_code.py` does not already serve as the A/B gate, and either (a) have the new script import and reuse `_median_gap` from the test module, or (b) extract the helper into a shared calibration utility both the test and the script call.
findings.finding: Section D's proposed `ab_impact_model.py` reinvents logic already present in-repo as a pinned production regression test. `tests/unit/test_impact_code.py:259-281` defines `_median_gap(fn)` and `test_impact_code_gap_beats_old_mean_gap` which asserts `_median_gap(impact_code) > _median_gap(impact)` on `code_review_impact_labels.jsonl` — the exact gate condition and fixture the A/B script is specified to provide. The plan does not cite this prior art or explain why a new standalone script is needed rather than a thin CLI wrapper around the existing test helper.
findings.location: Section D ('Diff-grounded A/B harness') / `docs/experiments/ab_impact_model.py` (new) / AC line 'exits non-zero unless the new model's HIGH↔NIT median separation strictly beats the baseline's'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (in the plan or AC) that the `--impact-model-version` filter skipping ALL legacy sidecars (which carry no `impact_model_version` key) is intentional and document it explicitly as the expected cold-start behavior.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py` already globs `.tickets-tracker/*/*-REVIEW_RESULT.json` and buckets by ticket — then proposes extending it with argparse — but does not acknowledge that the existing `load()` function uses `data.get('material_fingerprint')` as a sidecar key, while the proposed new filter checks `data.get('impact_model_version')`. Since `impact_model_version` is an entirely new top-level key that does not yet exist in any sidecar (no current sidecar emits it), the filter will skip ALL existing sidecars when `--impact-model-version` is supplied, which matches intent. However, the plan's back-compat claim ('absent the flag, behaviour is unchanged') is verifiable and confirmed, so this is informational rather than a gap — but the assertion that the existing script already works as described is confirmed by reading the file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify why `impact_model_version` is needed as a second versioning axis rather than bumping the existing `schema` version string (`plan_review_result_v1` → `v2`), or replace the new key with a schema bump and update `latest_review_result` / `calibrate_plan_review_thresholds.py` to gate on `schema`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a NEW top-level `impact_model_version` key as a second, parallel versioning dimension alongside the already-existing `schema` key (`plan_review_result_v1` / `code_review_result_v1`). The conventional prior art for marking a formula change that must be corpus-segmented is to bump the schema version string (the existing versioning axis), which sidecar readers already gate on (`payload.get('schema') == 'plan_review_result_v1'` in `latest_review_result`). The plan does not explain why a schema bump is insufficient — why the calibrator cannot filter on `schema == 'plan_review_result_v2'` instead — before committing to a second versioning layer that every sidecar reader must learn about.
findings.location: Section B ('Impact-model versioning') / both `sidecar.py` files / AC line 'Both `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecars stamp a top-level `impact_model_version`'
findings.suggested_fix: If the motivation is purely corpus segmentation (not a payload shape change), bump `SCHEMA` to `'plan_review_result_v2'` / `'code_review_result_v2'` and have the calibrator filter on `data.get('schema')`. This reuses the reader-guard already in `latest_review_result` and avoids a second versioning axis.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly assert (in the ADR or the scope section) that the impact-model scoring change and the `impact_model_version` sidecar-tagging change MUST be deployed in a single atomic commit/release, or add a fallback mechanism (e.g., a secondary signal such as a schema version or deploy timestamp) so that new-formula sidecars written during a tagging gap are not silently dropped.
findings.finding: The plan does not address the dual-write window where new-formula scores land in untagged sidecars during a rolling deploy: if the impact-model scoring change and the sidecar-writer tagging change are not deployed atomically, sidecars produced by the new formula but the old writer will lack `impact_model_version` and be silently skipped by the calibrator — causing real field observations to be dropped without warning, contaminating the version-v2 cohort with an invisible gap.
findings.location: Section B (Impact-model versioning) / Section C (Version-segmented calibration replay)
findings.suggested_fix: In `docs/adr/0036-impact-model-rollout-and-calibration-cadence.md`, add a deployment constraint: 'The sidecar tagging change (IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION constant + build_payload key) and the scoring-model change it versions MUST ship in the same release. Staggered or cherry-picked deploys of either half create untagged new-formula sidecars that the calibrator silently discards; treat any such window as a corpus gap and note it in the calibration run log.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
